utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: kelly wood . date of birth , february 15th , 1979 . established patient here for renal ultrasound because of hematuria . urine dipstick today . negative for leukocytes , nitrates , protein , ketone , bilirubin and glucose . color yellow and clear , urobilinogen 0.2 , ph 5.5 and specific gravity 1.020 . there is a trace amount of blood in the urine and intact . renal ultrasound impression . right kidney is 10 cm in length by five centimeter wide . there are no stones , masses or hydronephrosis . the left kidney is 10.8 centimeters in length and five centimeters wide . there is a six millimeter left renal stone non-obstructing . 533 milliliters pre-void and 0 milliliters post-void . hi , mrs. wood . i see you're just finished your ultrasound .

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: yes , ma'am .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: well , it looks like you have one stone in the left kidney but it's not obstructing anything .

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: i knew it had to be a kidney stone . i passed what looked like two small stones last week after i scheduled this appointment . and then for the past six weeks , i've had a few episodes of bright red urine the color of , like , cherry kool-aid . and last week , i was constantly having to pee and it hurt something terrible .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: how bad was your pain on a scale of zero to 10 ? zero being no pain and 10 being worse , as you can imagine .

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: um , at the time , i'd say an eight .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: are you having any flank pain now or tenderness in your lower back ?

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: no , after passing those stones , things have felt a lot better .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: that's good to hear . now , it looks like you're only taking alavert 10 milligrams a day and have no drug allergies . is that correct ?

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: that's right .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: all right , let's take a look at you today . let's use my default pe . well , mrs. wood , we know that the blood in your urine was due to the kidney stones . fortunately , you passed two last week and the blood has nearly subsided . you do still have one stone in the left kidney . since it's not obstructing or causing pain , i do n't recommend any surgical intervention at this time .

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: i'd like to know what i can do to stop getting these stones .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: as we discussed last time , the formation of kidney stones can not be attributed to a particular cause . but several factors increase the risk , such as dehydration , family history , certain foods , digestive tract disease and certain medical conditions .

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: yeah , i know i do n't drink near the amount of water that i should . i drink more mountain dew than anything .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: you should drink about half of your body weight in ounces of water a day .

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: so , if i weight 250 pounds , i should drink ?

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: that would be 125 ounces of water a day and you should avoid drinking sugary drinks like mountain dew .

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: okay . is there any food i should n't eat ?

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: great question . you should avoid food rich in sodium oxalate and animal protein . so that would be salty , processed food because they're high in sodium . ideally , you should consume less than 1,000 milligrams of sodium a day and reduce the amount of beef , poultry , fish , and pork .

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: what's oxalate ?

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: it's an organic acid found in plants , that when digested , it binds to minerals that stones are formed from . foods like spinach , chocolate , beets , rhubarb and soy products .

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: well , i'll do whatever it takes to try to avoid getting these stones again .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: great . and here's a handout that you can look over . it goes over the dietary recommendations and what to avoid .

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: okay , good . i like to have things in writing to help me remember .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: perfect . well , if you have any more questions . if you do n't have any more questions , you're all set . i wo n't need to see you back until next year , as long as you do n't have any more issues like bleeding or pain . if you do have any issues , you can always call the office sooner . and if you experience any severe pain or bright red blood in your urine , you should go to the emergency room .

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: all right . well , let's hope we do n't have to do either of those and i see you next year .

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: yes , let's hope . come right this way and i'll walk you to check out . update pe abdomen . no flank pain . anti gu . normal vaginal exam . primary diagnosis is hematuria . secondary diagnosis is chronic urol ... urolithiasis . thank you . this completes mrs. wood encounter .

detail_list.summary: The patient is a 41-year-old female with chronic urolithiasis presenting for follow-up regarding hematuria.

The patient's urine dipstick results are negative for leukocytes, nitrates, protein, ketone, bilirubin, and glucose, with a yellow/clear appearance, 0.2 urobilinogen, 5.5 pH, 1.020 specific gravity, and trace intact blood.
Renal ultrasound shows the right kidney is 10x5 cm with no stones, masses, or hydronephrosis, and the left kidney is 10.8x5 cm with a 6 mm non-obstructing stone.
Urolithiasis
The patient passed two small stones last week and experienced episodes of gross hematuria and pain over the past six weeks.
The doctor asks the patient to rate their pain on a scale of zero to 10.
The patient reports their pain level was an eight out of 10.
The doctor asks if the patient is currently experiencing flank pain or lower back tenderness.
The patient denies current flank pain or tenderness.
The patient takes Alavert 10 mg daily.
The doctor confirms the patient has no known drug allergies.
The doctor states that no surgical intervention is recommended for the remaining non-obstructing left renal stone.
The patient asks for advice on preventing future kidney stones.
The doctor recommends the patient drink half their body weight in ounces of water daily.
The doctor advises the patient to drink 125 ounces of water daily and avoid sugary drinks.
The patient asks if there are specific foods they should avoid.
The doctor recommends avoiding foods rich in sodium, oxalate, and animal protein, specifically limiting sodium to under 1,000 mg daily.
The patient asks for a definition of oxalate.
The doctor explains that oxalate is an organic acid found in plants like spinach, chocolate, beets, rhubarb, and soy that binds to minerals to form stones.
The doctor provides a handout with dietary recommendations and avoidances.
The doctor instructs the patient to follow up in one year with a renal ultrasound, or sooner if symptoms arise, and to go to the emergency room for severe pain or gross hematuria.
The doctor performs a physical exam confirming no flank pain and a normal vaginal exam.
